subject: otolaryngology
title: Ear injuries
preambleMarkdown:
# Ear injuries



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ear injuries is a clinical condition or syndrome in otolaryngology.
- This article focuses on ear, nose, throat, airway, hearing, vestibular, sinus, and head-neck pathology.
- The practical clinical priority is otolaryngology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Ear injuries to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ear injuries is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is otolaryngology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Ear injuries depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In otolaryngology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Ear injuries.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Ear injuries disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Ear injuries should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Ear injuries first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Ear injuries, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Ear injuries, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused otolaryngology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Ear injuries presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Ear injuries with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Ear injuries is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Ear injuries should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Ear injuries is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses otolaryngology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Ear injuries, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Ear injuries may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Ear injuries patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Ear injuries, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Ear injuries in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Ear injuries, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Ear injuries does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Ear injuries may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Ear injuries, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Ear injuries as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Ear injuries draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Ear

_Source lines: 23160-23507._

```text
II. EAR
A. Infections
1. Otitis externa
a. Discussion
i. Definition
a) Otitis externa is inflammation of the auditory canal.

b) Necrotizing (malignant) otitis externa is a severe form of otitis
externa that involves the surrounding tissues. It is almost always
caused by Pseudomonas and occurs in patients with diabetes and
other debilitating diseases.

ii. Etiology. Otitis externa is usually caused by a bacterial infection (most
often Pseudomonas , Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, or a gram-negative
rod). Other, less common causes include fungal infection (most com-
monly Aspergillus) and eczema.

iii. Predisposing factors include trauma to the epithelium; swimming; hot,
humid weather; cotton ear swabs; and the use of a hearing aid.

b. Clinical features
i. Symptoms include otalgia, pruritus, plugging of the ear, and decreased
hearing.

ii. Physical examination findings
a) The canal is erythematous, often with a purulent discharge.

b) The preauricular lymph nodes may be swollen.

c) When the tragus pinna is pulled to open the canal for visualization,
there is often pain.

c. Differential diagnoses include eczematous otitis externa, necrotizing otitis
externa, otitis media, mastoiditis, and a foreign body.

d. Evaluation. Diagnosis is usually made on the basis of the physical examina-
tion. The only time additional testing is warranted is if necrotizing otitis
externa is suspected; in this situation, a CT scan to evaluate the deep tissues
may be appropriate.

e. Therapy
i. Cleansing. The canal should be cleaned of debris by gentle irrigation
with tepid water or 2% acetic acid solution (vinegar).

ii. Antimicrobial therapy
a) Uncomplicated otitis externa. A topical antibiotic with steroids
(e.g., polymyxin B otic suspension) should be instilled in the ear
(four drops three times daily for 10 days).

b) Necrotizing external otitis media is treated intravenously with
ciprofloxacin (400 mg every 12 hours) and in this instance also used
in pediatric patients.

c) Fungal infection. Amphotericin B should be used in consultation
with an infectious disease specialist.

288 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Eye, Ear, Nose, Throat, and Dental Emergencies
f. Disposition. Patients with uncomplicated otitis externa can be sent
home with instructions to follow up with a primary care physician
in 3 to 5 days. The ear should be kept clean and dry, and the patient
should be educated about minimizing use of cotton swabs. Patients with
necrotizing otitis externa must be admitted for intravenous antibiotic
therapy.

2. Otitis media
a. Discussion
i. Definition. Otitis media is a bacterial infection of the middle ear.

ii. Etiology. The organisms that usually cause otitis media are
S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, Moraxella catarrhalis, and group A
streptococci; however, the incidences of each are changing due to
changes in the pediatric immunization schedule.

iii. Predisposing factors. Otitis media is often preceded by a viral upper
respiratory infection that produces Eustachian tube dysfunction sec-
ondary to swelling of the mucous membranes. The middle ear then fills
with fluid, which becomes infected with bacteria.

b. Clinical features
i. Symptoms
a) In young patients, the only symptoms may be fever and irritability.

Infants may bat at or play with the affected ear.

b) Older children and adults complain of pain and fullness in the ear
and trouble hearing.

ii. Physical examination findings
a) Fever is often present but not necessary for diagnosis.

b) The tympanic membrane will be bulging and erythematous and is
often yellow in color. A poor light reflex on the tympanic membrane
is noted. If the eardrum has ruptured, pus may be noted in the canal.

Pneumatic insufflation reveals an immobile tympanic membrane.

c. Differential diagnoses include otitis externa, malignant otitis externa, and
a foreign body. If there is swelling behind the ear, mastoiditis should be
considered.

d. Evaluation. In general, no additional testing is needed. If a pediatric patient
appears very ill or dehydrated, then additional workup may be warranted to
rule out septicemia and bacteremia.

e. Therapy
i. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are indicated to treat fever and
pain.

ii. Antibiotics
a) Children. Most infections are viral. Older than age 4, clinicians may
perform a watchful waiting where no antibiotics are started in the
first 48 hours. If treating immediately or the patient is still symptom-
atic after 48 hours, acceptable regimens include:
1) Amoxicillin (90 mg/kg, divided every 12 hours), the drug of
choice, except in patients who are allergic to penicillin and those
with recently recurrent infections
2) Azithromycin (10 mg/kg on day 1, 5 mg/kg on days 2 to 5)
3) Cefdinir (14 mg/kg/day, divided every 12 hours)
4) Ceftriaxone (50 mg/kg intramuscularly)
b) Adults. Acceptable regimens include:
1) Amoxicillin (500 mg every 12 hours or 875 mg every 12 hours in
more complicated infections; treatment of choice in nonallergic
patients)
2) Cefdinir (300 mg every 12 hours)
3) Azithromycin (500 mg on day 1, 250 mg on days 2 to 5)
f. Disposition
i. Discharge. Most patients can be discharged with instructions to return
to a primary care physician in 2 weeks for a follow-up examination.

However, if the patient still has a fever or other symptoms after 3 days
E Y E , E A R , N O S E , T H R O A T , A N D D E N T A L E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	289

Eye, Ear, Nose, Throat, and Dental Emergencies
of therapy, a return visit to the ED or follow-up with the patient's own
physician is advised.

ii. Admission. Very ill or dehydrated children with otitis may require ad-
mission to the hospital.

## 3. Mastoiditis

a. Discussion. Mastoiditis is an inflammatory process in the mastoid air cells.

i. Acute mastoiditis is a suppurative process that is usually preceded by
an acute episode of otitis media.

ii. Chronic mastoiditis is usually associated with a cholesteatoma or
chronic ear disease.

b. Clinical features
i. Symptoms include otalgia, fever, and pain behind the ear. Hearing may
be impaired.

ii. Physical examination findings
a) Fever is almost always present.

b) The tympanic membrane is bulging and erythematous, except when
perforation has occurred; with perforation, otorrhea is seen.

c) Postauricular erythema, edema, swelling, and tenderness may be
seen.

d) Altered mental status or meningeal signs may be present.

c. Differential diagnoses include otitis externa, malignant otitis externa, otitis
media, and a foreign body.

d. Evaluation
i. Laboratory studies. A complete blood count (CBC) shows elevation of
the white blood cell count with a left shift in the differential.

ii. Imaging studies. CT scan may reveal thickening of the mastoid air cell
epithelial membrane, air-fluid levels, and a subperiosteal abscess.

iii. Other studies. In a patient with altered mental status or signs of
meningeal irritation and no evidence of an abscess on the CT scan, a
lumbar puncture should be performed to rule out meningitis, which
can result from progression of the mastoiditis. Immediate antibiotic
therapy directed at an abscess or meningitis (ceftriaxone or cefotaxime
in children and adults and high-dose penicillin in elderly patients)
should be instituted without awaiting the results of testing.

e. Therapy
i. Antibiotic therapy
a) Adults. Appropriate regimens include intravenous vancomycin
(15 to 20 mg/kg daily and follow levels) and ceftriaxone (2 g every
12 hours).

b) Children. Appropriate regimens include intravenous vancomycin
(60 mg/kg/day divided every 6 hours) plus one of the following if
history of recurrent otitis media:
1) Ceftazidime (150 mg/kg/day, divided every 8 hours)
2) Cefepime (150 mg/kg/day, divided every 8 hours)
3) Piperacillin-tazobactam (300 mg/kg/day divided every 8 hours)
ii. Myringotomy with tube placement is performed by an
otolaryngologist.

a) Following the procedure, daily cleaning of the ear is necessary to
ensure patency.

b) Topical antibiotics (e.g., polymyxin B otic suspension) are often in-
stilled after cleaning.

iii. Mastoidectomy is reserved for patients who do not respond to treat-
ment and patients with meningeal signs.

f. Disposition. Admission to the hospital and consultation with an
otolaryngologist in the ED are indicated.

B. Foreign body
1. Discussion. Foreign bodies in the ear are a common presenting complaint in
the ED. Children and mentally handicapped patients often present with objects
Quick 	HIT
An auricle that is protruded
forward, causing the ear to
"stick out" should be con-
sidered a warning sign for
mastoiditis.

290 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Eye, Ear, Nose, Throat, and Dental Emergencies
such as beads, erasers, and small toys lodged in the ear canal. Insects in the
ear, usually cockroaches and often alive, are also seen in a number of cases.

2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms
i. If the foreign body has been present for a relatively long time, the pre-
senting complaint may be discharge from the ear.

ii. Live insects can cause pain, and patients are often anxious to have
something done immediately.

b. Physical examination findings
i. Signs of otitis externa may be present if the foreign body has been in the
ear for some time.

ii. The object may be impacted in cerumen. A live or dead insect may
be seen.

3. Differential diagnoses include otitis externa, malignant otitis externa, and
otitis media with perforation.

4. Evaluation is by physical examination.

## 5. Therapy

a. Object removal. Children need to be restrained or sedated to prevent
struggling while attempting removal.

i. Irrigation. Often, objects can be flushed out with warm water and
an irrigating bulb or a syringe attached to an 18-gauge angiographic
catheter.

ii. Alligator forceps can be used to remove objects. Care should be used
not to perforate the tympanic membrane. Beads often have a hole and
can be rotated to allow easier grasping with the forceps.

iii. A whistle-tip suction catheter can sometimes be used to remove beads
or other round objects. Care should be taken to avoid indiscriminate
suctioning, which can lead to perforation of the tympanic membrane.

b. Supportive care. Reexamination after the object is removed may reveal
abrasions, otitis externa, or both, in which case proper therapy should be
instituted.

6. Disposition. In most cases, patients can be discharged with instructions to
follow-up with a primary care physician in 3 to 5 days. If attempts in the ED
to remove the object are unsuccessful, the object may need to be surgically re-
moved, which requires admission.

C. Tympanic membrane rupture. Disarticulation of the ossicles and rupture of the
round or oval windows may be seen in addition to tympanic membrane rupture.

## 1. Etiology

a. Increased pressure in the external ear canal, such as can result from a slap
to the ear, while scuba diving, or from an explosion, can lead to rupture of
the tympanic membrane.

b. Penetrating injuries can result from overambitious cleaning of the external
ear canal with a cotton swab and from flying debris (e.g., explosions, weld-
ing sparks).

2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms include otalgia, hearing loss, and bloody otorrhea.

b. Physical examination findings
i. The perforation may range from small and linear to stellate.

ii. Inflammation of the underlying epithelium may occur as a result of in-
fection. Infection is more likely to occur in association with diving and
welding accidents.

iii. Rinne-Weber (tuning fork) hearing tests reveal conductive hearing loss.

Bone conduction is better than air conduction.

iv. Evidence of vertigo or spontaneous nystagmus must be sought. These
findings could represent a more serious injury to the inner ear.

3. Evaluation is by physical examination. In patients with evidence of vertigo,
severe hearing loss, or facial nerve palsy, immediate consultation with an oto-
laryngologist is indicated.

Quick 	HIT
Children often deny the
presence of a foreign body
because they are afraid of
punishment.

Quick 	HIT
If the foreign body is a live in-
sect, mineral oil or lidocaine
solution can be instilled in
the ear to kill the insect prior
to attempting removal.

E Y E , E A R , N O S E , T H R O A T , A N D D E N T A L E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	291

Eye, Ear, Nose, Throat, and Dental Emergencies

## 4. Therapy

a. Simple, noncontaminated perforations. The patient should be advised to
keep water out of the affected ear until the perforation heals spontaneously.

b. Simple, contaminated perforations are treated with ofloxacin. Patients
should be advised to avoid getting water in the affected ear.

c. Complicated perforations. Patients with marked destruction of the tym-
panic membrane and debris in the middle ear should be seen by an otolar-
yngologist for debridement.

5. Disposition. Most patients can be discharged with instructions to follow up with
an otolaryngologist. If marked destruction of the tympanic membrane or vertigo
is present, consultation with an otolaryngologist in the ED is appropriate.
```
